title: EIP-8025: Optional Execution Proofs
description: Introduces optional execution proofs on the consensus layer, allowing beacon nodes to verify execution payload validity within beacon blocks without running an execution layer client. Proofs are generated by zkEVM proof-generating nodes and propagated over the CL peer-to-peer network, enabling stateless validation as an opt-in mode for validators.
author: Kevaundray Wedderburn (@kevaundray), Justin Drake (@JustinDrake) <justin@ethereum.org>
laymanDescription: Lets validators verify blocks using a compact math proof instead of re-running every transaction. This is opt-in, so validators can choose to skip running a full execution client, lowering their hardware and bandwidth needs without changing how the network reaches consensus.

endUsers.description: No direct impact. End users are unaffected as this is an opt-in validator infrastructure change with no effect on transaction processing.

appDevs.description: No impact. Application developers face no protocol changes; execution semantics and smart contract behavior remain identical.

walletDevs.description: No impact. Wallet interfaces and transaction flows are unchanged by this consensus-layer-only validator optimization.

toolingInfra.description: Monitoring tools may want to track zkEVM proof generation and propagation metrics on the new CL subnets for observability.

layer2s.description: No direct impact. Layer 2 protocols do not interact with CL proof propagation; future gas limit increases enabled by this work could indirectly benefit L2s.

stakersNodes.description: Validators gain an optional stateless validation mode, potentially reducing hardware and bandwidth requirements for block verification.

clClients.description: Consensus clients must implement new P2P subnets for proof propagation, zkEVM proof verification logic, and the stateless validation mode.

elClients.description: No direct changes required. EL clients continue to function normally; stateless validators simply bypass the EL verification path.

benefits: Reduces hardware and bandwidth requirements for validators opting into stateless verification.
Decouples block verification cost from gas limit, supporting future gas limit increases.
Provides a safe, opt-in testing ground for execution proofs without making them consensus critical.
Enables validators to attest without running a full execution layer client.
tradeoffs: Proof generation requires significant computational resources from dedicated zkEVM proof-generating nodes.
Stateless validators depend on timely proof availability from the P2P network.

scaleL1.description: Verification cost no longer grows with the gas limit, enabling higher throughput without proportionally increasing validator hardware requirements.
